kata.title: Triangle
kata.workload: build one Vec[Vec[i64]] triangle of N=200 rows ONCE, then run the O(N^2) bottom-up rolling min-path DP dp[k]=triangle[i][k]+min(dp[k],dp[k+1]) K=20000 times with a data-dependent seed (seed=acc%97, non-uniform base-row perturbation (seed+j)%7 so the winning path shifts each rep and the DP can't be hoisted), folding every result — the rolling-1D min-DP over read-only nested indexing regime

env.note: Measured on a shared x86-64 Linux cloud container (karac from kara main), NOT the corpus's Apple M5 Pro. Canonical M5 numbers in the sibling results.json are left untouched. Absolute times/sizes/RSS NOT comparable across the two files; only within-file cross-language ratios are the signal. Seq lane (fastest first): c 363ms ~ kara 367ms < go 564ms < rust 574ms < rust_ovf 653ms. RESULT: kara TIES C (1.01x) and is ~1.56x AHEAD of rustc -O (and 1.78x ahead of equal-safety rust_ovf) EVEN THOUGH kara keeps both overflow AND bounds checks. Mechanism: kara's bounds-check elision proves the nested index triangle[i][k] / dp[k] / dp[k+1] in-range from the `while k <= i` guard and drops all three inner checks (branchless cmov min + one jo overflow, no bounds panic; ~11 panic sites in the whole binary), while idiomatic Rust keeps the nested-Vec<Vec> bounds checks (~300 panic sites). The overflow tax is NOT the gap (rust_ovf is only ~14% behind rust -O; both trail kara). All compiled mirrors agree on the sink; correctness verified across interp/JIT/AOT/auto-par and valgrind-clean. Python runs a smaller K (own sink, not cross-checked). Re-run bench.sh on the M5 to fold comparable numbers into the corpus tables.

runtime.note: K=2000, own sink 766597945, timed separately, NOT cross-checked
